then every year you don't do it, you

if you had made that investment. And so,
the The key point here is that we use
poor people language when we talk about
education
when we should be using investor
language because it should be an
investment in an asset, and the asset
just happens to be you, and you can't
sell it. But you can invest in it and
you can grow it. If you think about
investments, you'll never say an
investment is expensive. There's just a
cost and a return. But, if someone says
there's a $100 stock or a $1,000 stock,
it could be expensive relative to the
return, but if the return is supposed to
be good, then no one says that that
amount of money itself is expensive.
It's just what the cost is for the
return. It is the investment. And so, if
you think about education that same way,
it always has to be framed in terms of
what you get for it, not how much it
costs. It's not about how much you make
now, it's how much you're going to make
and how much that's worth to you. And
for me, whatever my delta is is what I'm

probably I mean, shoot, I lost

like I have always been

this
this spell that it would only take in

in what way will this make me better?
And so, by instead asking that question

"There is something that I can learn

one of the most valuable frames that

The thing is is that there's a gap here,

the people who already bought that brick
